seoTitle: How to Verify Domain Ownership in Webflow
metaDescription: Confirm domain ownership in Webflow by adding a TXT verification record to your registrar's DNS settings for SSL activation.
intro: Before Webflow will fully activate hosting and SSL on a custom domain, it needs to confirm you actually own it. This guide shows where to find your unique TXT verification string and how to add it correctly to your domain registrar's DNS settings. It's for anyone completing a domain connection or handing off a finished site to a client.

steps.heading: Open the Custom Domains settings
steps.body: In your Webflow project, go to the production-level Custom Domains settings to locate your domain configuration.

steps.heading: Copy your verification token
steps.body: Find the one-time TXT record string Webflow generates for validating ownership of your domain.

steps.heading: Log into your domain registrar
steps.body: Open your external DNS management dashboard, wherever your domain is actually registered.

steps.heading: Add the TXT record
steps.body: Create a new TXT record using your domain's hostname and paste in the exact verification value from Webflow.

steps.heading: Confirm verification and check SSL
steps.body: Return to Webflow and click the verify domain status button, then run a DNS propagation check to confirm SSL certificate activation.

faq.question: Why won't my domain verify immediately?
faq.answer: DNS changes take time to propagate, so it can take anywhere from a few minutes to a day before Webflow recognizes the new TXT record.

faq.question: Should I remove old verification records?
faq.answer: Yes, leftover duplicate TXT records from a previous configuration can cause conflicts, so clean those up first.

faq.question: Does verifying ownership also set up hosting?
faq.answer: Verification confirms you control the domain, but you'll still need to add the A and CNAME records separately to route traffic to Webflow.

faq.question: What if I don't have access to my registrar's DNS settings?
faq.answer: You'll need to work with whoever manages your domain's DNS, since the TXT record must be added there directly.
